content:
# Acts Chapter 7 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 232-234 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_007_page_001.jpg, ACT_007_page_002.jpg, ACT_007_page_003.jpg

---

**1** And the high priest said, "Are these things so?"

**2** And he said, "Brothers and fathers, hear me. The God of glory appeared to our father Abraham when he was in Mesopotamia, before he settled in Haran,

**3** and said to him, 'Go out from your land and from your kindred, and come into the land which I will show you.'

**4** Then he went out from the land of the Chaldeans and settled in Haran. And from there, after his father died, God removed him into this land in which you now dwell.

**5** And he gave him no inheritance in it, not even a foot's length, but he promised to give it to him as a possession, and to his offspring after him, though he had no child.

**6** And God spoke thus: that his offspring would be sojourners in a foreign land, and the people there would enslave them and mistreat them four hundred years.

**7** 'And the nation which they serve I will judge,' said God, 'and after these things they shall come out and worship me in this place.'

**8** And he gave him the covenant of circumcision; and so Abraham begot Isaac and circumcised him on the eighth day, and Isaac begot Jacob, and Jacob the twelve patriarchs.

**9** And the patriarchs, consumed with jealousy, sold Joseph into Egypt; but God was with him

**10** and rescued him out of all his afflictions, and gave him favor and wisdom before Pharaoh, king of Egypt, who appointed him ruler over Egypt and over all his household.

**11** Then a famine came over all Egypt and Canaan, and great affliction, and our fathers could not find food.

**12** But when Jacob heard that there was grain in Egypt, he sent our fathers the first time.

**13** And on the second visit, Joseph was made known to his brothers, and Joseph's family became known to Pharaoh.

**14** And Joseph sent word and summoned his father Jacob and all his kindred, seventy-five souls in all.

**15** And Jacob went down into Egypt, and he died, he and our fathers,

**16** and they were carried over to Shechem and laid in the tomb that Abraham had bought for a sum of silver from the sons of Hamor in Shechem.

**17** But as the time of the promise drew near, which God had promised to Abraham, the people grew and multiplied in Egypt,

**18** until there arose another king over Egypt who did not know Joseph.

**19** This one dealt craftily with our race and mistreated our fathers, forcing them to expose their infants so that they would not survive.

**20** At that time Moses was born, and he was beautiful before God; and he was nourished three months in his father's house.

**21** And when he was exposed, the daughter of Pharaoh took him up and raised him as her own son.

**22** And Moses was educated in all the wisdom of the Egyptians, and he was mighty in his words and his deeds.

**23** And when a period of forty years was fulfilled for him, it came into his heart to visit his brothers, the sons of Israel.

**24** And seeing one of them being wronged, he defended him and avenged the one being oppressed, striking down the Egyptian.

**25** He supposed that his brothers would understand that God was giving them deliverance through his hand, but they did not understand.

**26** And on the following day he appeared to them as they were fighting and tried to reconcile them, saying, 'Men, you are brothers; why do you wrong one another?'

**27** But the one who was wronging his neighbor thrust him aside, saying, 'Who appointed you a ruler and a judge over us?

**28** Do you wish to kill me as you killed the Egyptian yesterday?'

**29** And Moses fled at this word and became a sojourner in the land of Midian, where he fathered two sons.

**30** And when forty years had passed, an angel appeared to him in the wilderness of Mount Sinai, in a flame of fire in a bush.

**31** And when Moses saw it, he marveled at the sight, and as he drew near to look, there came a voice of the Lord:

**32** 'I am the God of your fathers, the God of Abraham and of Isaac and of Jacob.' And Moses trembled and did not dare to look.

**33** And the Lord said to him, 'Remove the sandal from your feet, for the place on which you stand is holy ground.

**34** I have surely seen the mistreatment of my people who are in Egypt, and I have heard their groaning, and I have come down to deliver them. And now come, I will send you to Egypt.'

**35** This Moses whom they rejected, saying, 'Who appointed you a ruler and a judge?' — this one God sent as both ruler and redeemer, by the hand of the angel who appeared to him in the bush.

**36** This one led them out, performing wonders and signs in the land of Egypt and at the Red Sea and in the wilderness for forty years.

**37** This is the Moses who said to the sons of Israel, 'God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among your brothers.'

**38** This is the one who was in the assembly in the wilderness, with the angel who spoke to him at Mount Sinai, and with our fathers — the one who received living oracles to give to us,

**39** to whom our fathers were unwilling to be obedient, but thrust him aside and turned in their hearts back to Egypt,

**40** saying to Aaron, 'Make for us gods who will go before us; for this Moses who led us out from the land of Egypt — we do not know what has happened to him.'

**41** And they made a calf in those days and brought a sacrifice to the idol, and were rejoicing in the works of their hands.

**42** But God turned away and gave them over to worship the host of heaven, as it is written in the book of the prophets: 'Did you bring to me slain offerings and sacrifices during the forty years in the wilderness, O house of Israel?

**43** And you took up the tent of Moloch and the star of the god Rephan, the images which you made to worship them; and I will remove you beyond Babylon.'

**44** The tent of the testimony was among our fathers in the wilderness, just as the one who spoke to Moses directed him to make it according to the pattern which he had seen.

**45** And our fathers who received it in turn brought it in with Joshua when they dispossessed the nations whom God drove out before our fathers, until the days of David,

**46** who found favor before God and asked to find a dwelling place for the house of Jacob.

**47** But Solomon built him a house.

**48** Yet the Most High does not dwell in things made by hands, as the prophet says:

**49** 'Heaven is my throne, and the earth is a footstool for my feet. What kind of house will you build for me, says the Lord, or what is the place of my rest?

**50** Did not my hand make all these things?'

**51** You stiff-necked and uncircumcised in hearts and ears! You always resist the Holy Spirit — as your fathers did, so do you!

**52** Which of the prophets did your fathers not persecute? And they killed those who announced beforehand the coming of the Righteous One, of whom you have now become betrayers and murderers —

**53** you who received the law as delivered by angels and did not keep it."

**54** Now when they heard these things, they were cut to their hearts, and they gnashed their teeth at him.

**55** But being full of the Holy Spirit, he gazed intently into heaven and saw the glory of God, and Jesus standing at the right hand of God,

**56** and he said, "Behold, I see the heavens opened and the Son of Man standing at the right hand of God!"

**57** But they cried out with a loud voice and stopped their ears, and they rushed upon him with one accord.

**58** And casting him out of the city, they began to stone him. And the witnesses laid down their garments at the feet of a young man named Saul.

**59** And as they were stoning Stephen, he called out, "Lord Jesus, receive my spirit!"

**60** And falling on his knees, he cried out with a loud voice, "Lord, do not hold this sin against them!" And having said this, he fell asleep.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

This chapter contains Stephen's great defense speech before the Sanhedrin — the longest speech in Acts — and his subsequent martyrdom. It is a masterpiece of early Christian rhetoric, tracing Israel's history from Abraham through Moses to Solomon, building an argument that God's people have repeatedly rejected God's chosen agents, culminating in the betrayal of "the Righteous One." The consensus text draws primarily on the formal precision of LOGOS, enriched at key points by EMET's literalness and PNEUMA's clarity.

### Key Decisions

1. **Verse 2 — Address formula:** EMET preserved the full Greek "Men, brothers and fathers" (andres adelphoi kai pateres). LOGOS and PNEUMA simplified to "Brothers and fathers." The consensus follows the more natural English of LOGOS/PNEUMA, as "Men" here is a vocative filler, not a gendered address.

2. **Verse 9 — "consumed with jealousy":** PNEUMA's intensified rendering captures the force of the Greek aorist participle zelosantes better than EMET's "having-been-jealous" or LOGOS's "being jealous." The jealousy was the motive force, and the Greek participle carries causal weight.

3. **Verse 14 — Seventy-five souls:** All three translators correctly follow the Sinaiticus reading of 75 (hebdomekonta pente), which follows the LXX tradition. The manuscript is clear on this point. The Masoretic Text reads 70; Stephen, quoting the Septuagint, says 75. No divergence among translators.

4. **Verse 20 — "beautiful before God":** EMET: "beautiful to-God"; LOGOS: "beautiful before God"; PNEUMA: "no ordinary child." The consensus follows LOGOS's formal rendering, which preserves the divine perspective (asteios to theo) while remaining intelligible. PNEUMA's dynamic rendering, while vivid, loses the explicit reference to God.

5. **Verse 25 — "deliverance" vs. "salvation":** EMET and LOGOS used "salvation" (soterian); PNEUMA used "deliver them." The consensus uses "deliverance," which captures the soteriological meaning while keeping the immediate context of physical rescue.

6. **Verse 33 — Singular "sandal":** The Sinaiticus clearly reads the singular to hupodema. EMET correctly preserved "the sandal" (singular). LOGOS wrote "sandals" (plural), deviating from the manuscript. PNEUMA also wrote "sandals" (plural). The consensus follows EMET and the manuscript: "the sandal" (singular), as Sinaiticus reads. This is a notable textual point — the singular matches the LXX of Exodus 3:5.

7. **Verse 34 — Hebrew doubling construction:** EMET: "Having-seen, I-saw"; PNEUMA: "I have seen — I have truly seen"; LOGOS: "I have surely seen." The consensus follows LOGOS's rendering "I have surely seen," which captures the Hebraic infinitive absolute (idon eidon) concisely. PNEUMA's dramatic repetition is also effective but slightly over-extended for a consensus text.

8. **Verse 43 — "beyond Babylon":** All three translators correctly read "Babylon" from Sinaiticus, following the LXX of Amos 5:27. The Hebrew Masoretic Text of Amos reads "Damascus." Stephen (or his LXX source) updated the geography to reflect the Babylonian exile. No divergence.

9. **Verse 46 — "house of Jacob" vs. "God of Jacob":** This is the major textual variant in the chapter. Sinaiticus reads to oiko Iakob ("for the house of Jacob"). Vaticanus and other witnesses read to theo Iakob ("for the God of Jacob"), following Psalm 132:5 LXX. EMET correctly read "house of Jacob" from Sinaiticus. LOGOS and PNEUMA both read "God of Jacob." The manuscript image on page 233 confirms the Sinaiticus reading as oiko (house). However, the "God of Jacob" reading has strong external support (Vaticanus, several majuscules). The consensus follows Sinaiticus and EMET: "house of Jacob," since our base text is Sinaiticus, but this variant is logged as a significant disagreement.

10. **Verse 51 — "stiff-necked":** EMET and LOGOS preserved the literal "stiff-necked" (sklerotracheloi). PNEUMA rendered it "stubborn people." The consensus retains "stiff-necked," as it is a recognizable biblical idiom (cf. Exodus 32:9, 33:3) and preserves the physicality of Stephen's invective — he is using covenant language against covenant people.

11. **Verse 58 — "garments" vs. "cloaks":** EMET: "garments"; LOGOS: "garments"; PNEUMA: "cloaks." The consensus follows the majority: "garments" (himatia), the standard term for outer clothing.

12. **Verse 60 — "he fell asleep":** All three translators preserved this crucial euphemism (ekoimethee). The gentle verb for death stands in deliberate contrast to the violence of the stoning, carrying resurrection hope. Full agreement among all translators.

---

## Disagreement Log

### Verse 2 — Opening Address
- **EMET:** "Men, brothers and fathers" (full literal Greek)
- **LOGOS:** "Brothers and fathers"
- **PNEUMA:** "Brothers and fathers"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA. The vocative andres is a rhetorical filler that sounds archaic and misleading in English.

### Verse 9 — Jealousy of the Patriarchs
- **EMET:** "having-been-jealous"
- **LOGOS:** "being jealous"
- **PNEUMA:** "consumed with jealousy"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts PNEUMA's "consumed with jealousy," which captures the causal force of the Greek aorist participle more vividly than the other renderings.

### Verse 20 — Moses' Beauty
- **EMET:** "beautiful to-God"
- **LOGOS:** "beautiful before God"
- **PNEUMA:** "no ordinary child"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. The phrase asteios to theo demands a rendering that includes the divine reference. PNEUMA's dynamic rendering, while capturing the sense, drops the explicit mention of God.

### Verse 25 — Salvation/Deliverance
- **EMET:** "salvation"
- **LOGOS:** "salvation"
- **PNEUMA:** "deliver them"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA uses "deliverance" — a noun that bridges the soteriological weight of EMET/LOGOS with the concrete physical rescue PNEUMA emphasizes.

### Verse 33 — Singular vs. Plural Sandal(s)
- **EMET:** "the sandal of your feet" (singular, following Sinaiticus)
- **LOGOS:** "the sandals from your feet" (plural)
- **PNEUMA:** "the sandals off your feet" (plural)
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET and the manuscript: "the sandal from your feet" (singular). The Sinaiticus clearly reads to hupodema (singular). This matches the LXX of Exodus 3:5. LOGOS and PNEUMA appear to have harmonized with the more familiar plural form.

### Verse 34 — Hebraic Doubling
- **EMET:** "Having-seen, I-saw"
- **LOGOS:** "I have surely seen"
- **PNEUMA:** "I have seen — I have truly seen"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS's concise "I have surely seen," which captures the emphatic force of the Hebraic construction idon eidon without excessive elaboration.

### Verse 42 — "Slain victims/beasts/offerings"
- **EMET:** "slain-victims and sacrifices"
- **LOGOS:** "slain beasts and sacrifices"
- **PNEUMA:** "sacrifices and offerings"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA uses "slain offerings and sacrifices," blending the sense. The Greek sphagi kai thusiai distinguishes slaughtered animals from other types of sacrifice.

### Verse 46 — "House of Jacob" vs. "God of Jacob"
- **EMET:** "house of Jacob" (following Sinaiticus: to oiko Iakob)
- **LOGOS:** "God of Jacob" (following Vaticanus and Psalm 132:5 LXX)
- **PNEUMA:** "God of Jacob" (following Vaticanus and Psalm 132:5 LXX)
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET and the Sinaiticus manuscript: "house of Jacob." Our base text is Sinaiticus, and the manuscript image on page 233 confirms this reading. The variant "God of Jacob" (to theo Iakob) has strong support from Vaticanus and is the more familiar reading from Psalm 132:5, which may explain why LOGOS and PNEUMA adopted it. Both readings make theological sense: David asked to find a dwelling either for God's people (house of Jacob) or for God himself (God of Jacob). This is a genuine textual uncertainty and one of the most discussed variants in Acts 7.

### Verse 51 — "Stiff-necked" vs. "Stubborn"
- **EMET:** "Stiff-necked"
- **LOGOS:** "Stiff-necked"
- **PNEUMA:** "Stubborn people"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS majority: "stiff-necked." This is a deeply rooted biblical idiom (Exodus 32:9, 33:3; Deuteronomy 9:6) that Stephen deliberately invokes. PNEUMA's "stubborn" loses the covenant resonance.

### Verse 58 — "Garments" vs. "Cloaks"
- **EMET:** "garments"
- **LOGOS:** "garments"
- **PNEUMA:** "cloaks"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS majority: "garments" (himatia). While "cloaks" is not incorrect, "garments" is the more standard rendering of himatia.

---

*— SOPHIA*
